reason: This passage from Exodus 12 (the Passover account) explicitly demonstrates and emphasizes hierarchical structure at multiple levels. God calls Moses and Aaron specifically, establishing them as intermediaries between the divine and the people. The text repeatedly shows the people obeying commands transmitted through Moses and Aaron ('the people did as Moses commanded'). Additionally, the passage establishes categorical distinctions and hierarchical restrictions: only circumcised males may participate in the Passover; foreigners must be circumcised before partaking; hired workers and sojourners are excluded; purchased servants who are circumcised are included. These distinctions explicitly reinforce hierarchical and categorical divisions. The phrase 'Thus all the children of Israel did as the Lord commanded Moses and Aaron' (v. 50) affirms obedience to established authority. Far from discouraging hierarchy, the text presumes and reinforces it.
